content.text: The exhaustive-deps Lint Rule
The
react-hooks/exhaustive-deps
ESLint rule is part of the official
eslint-plugin-react-hooks
package. It statically analyses every
useEffect
useCallback
, and
useMemo

call in your code and verifies that the dependency array includes every reactive value used inside the callback. If any value is missing, it reports a warning. If there are unnecessary entries, it may also report them.

attrs.alt: exhaustive-deps ESLint rule: missing dep detection

content.text: Installation and Setup
The recommended configuration sets
exhaustive-deps
to
warn
. Many teams upgrade it to
error
to enforce it in CI. Either way, the rule runs on every file in your project that contains hooks.
What the Rule Detects
Missing Dependencies
The most important case: a reactive value used inside the callback but not listed in the dependency array.
Unnecessary Dependencies
Values that are always stable (state setters,
dispatch
, module-level constants) and do not need to be listed:
Unstable Object/Function Dependencies
Reading the Warning Messages
The rule produces specific, actionable messages. Learning to parse them quickly makes fixing them mechanical:
Message pattern
Meaning
Fix
'X' is missing from the dependency array
Value X is used inside the effect but not listed
Add X to the dep array, or restructure so X is not needed
'X' makes the dependencies change on every render
X is an object/array/function that's recreated each render
Destructure to primitives, move inside effect, or use useMemo/useCallback
React Hook useEffect has an unnecessary dependency: 'X'
X is listed but never used inside the callback
Remove X from the dep array
Either include it or remove the dependency array
Suggests options when a fix isn't obvious
Follow one of the two paths
The Correct Response to Each Warning
Case 1: Genuinely Missing Dependency
The simplest case — add it:
Case 2: Effect That Should Run Only Once

The warning appears, but you intentionally want a mount-only effect that reads a value once. The solution is not to silence the lint rule — it is to restructure so the effect genuinely has no reactive deps:
Case 3: Function Defined in the Component
When the Rule Suggests the Wrong Fix

Occasionally the lint rule suggests adding a dependency that would cause a re-run loop or other undesirable behaviour. In those cases, you may disable the rule for a single line — but only after verifying the code is correct:
Rule of thumb:
if you need to disable the lint rule, write a comment explaining exactly why. If you cannot explain why, that is a signal that the code should be restructured instead.
The Rule and useCallback / useMemo
The
exhaustive-deps
rule applies to all the hooks that take a dependency array, not just
useEffect
Configuring the Rule for Custom Hooks
If you write custom hooks that accept a callback and a dependency array (like the built-in hooks), you can configure
exhaustive-deps
to recognise them:
CI Integration
To enforce the rule in continuous integration:
How the Rule Works Internally
The
exhaustive-deps

rule is a static analysis tool that walks the AST (Abstract Syntax Tree) of the callback function. It identifies every identifier inside the callback that is not declared within the callback itself, and then checks whether each one is declared outside the component (stable), or inside the component body (reactive). Reactive values must appear in the dep array.
The rule knows about React's built-in stable values (
useState
setter,
useReducer
dispatch,
useRef
object) and excludes them from the missing-dep analysis automatically.
Edge Case: Accessing Object Properties

When you access a property of an object inside an effect, the rule treats the property access as a dependency on the object itself, not the specific property. This is conservative but sometimes overly strict.
Fixing Warnings Without Suppressing
Before reaching for an eslint-disable comment, try these restructuring patterns in order:
Move the value inside the effect if it's only used there.
Destructure to primitives if the object has stable primitive fields.
Use useMemo/useCallback to stabilise the reference.
Extract the logic into a helper that takes the value as an argument.
Use a ref to read the latest value without making it a reactive dep.
Autofix Support
The rule has limited autofix support. Running
eslint --fix
can automatically add missing dependencies in simple cases. However, autofixes for complex cases (unstable objects, functions) may not produce correct code — always review generated fixes.

# Run autofix
npx eslint src --fix --rule 'react-hooks/exhaustive-deps: warn'

# Autofixable: simple missing primitive dep
# Before: }, []);
# After:  }, [userId]);

# NOT autofixable: unstable object dep, function dep
# The rule will still warn — manual restructuring needed
TypeScript and the Rule
The
exhaustive-deps
rule works with TypeScript out of the box — the
eslint-plugin-react-hooks
package handles both JavaScript and TypeScript. However, TypeScript types do not affect the rule's analysis; it is purely a static flow analysis of identifiers and closures.
Summary
The
react-hooks/exhaustive-deps
ESLint rule is an automated tool that catches missing and unnecessary dependencies in hooks that accept dependency arrays. It is one of the highest-value lint rules in the React ecosystem.
Install eslint-plugin-react-hooks and enable exhaustive-deps (recommend 'warn' or 'error').
The rule detects: missing deps, unstable object/function deps that cause over-firing, and unused deps.
The correct response to a missing-dep warning is to add the dep or restructure the code — not silence it.
Use // eslint-disable-next-line only with a written justification; it is a last resort.
The rule also applies to useCallback and useMemo, not just useEffect.
Configure additionalHooks to extend the rule to your own custom hooks.
Add --max-warnings 0 in CI to treat exhaustive-deps warnings as errors.
